focus_ar: ربط الساكن بالمتحرك — وصل الكلمات بسلاسة
arabic_challenge: Arabic speakers often pronounce each word as a separate, complete unit with a small pause or glottal reset between words. Natural English speech instead links a word ending in a consonant directly onto the next word if it starts with a vowel, so the two words blend into what sounds like one longer word. Skipping this linking is one of the biggest reasons careful, grammatically-correct English can still sound choppy and non-native.

target_sounds: consonant + vowel linking across word boundaries

isolation.instructions: When a word ends in a consonant sound and the next word starts with a vowel sound, native speakers slide the consonant onto the front of the next word — no pause, no reset.
isolation.instructions_ar: لما كلمة تنتهي بصوت ساكن والكلمة اللي بعدها تبدأ بصوت متحرك، المتكلم الأصلي بيسحب الصوت الساكن لأول الكلمة اللي بعدها — بدون وقفة.

pair: a napple (what it sounds like — normal!)

sentence_practice: Pick up an apple and look at it.
Turn off the light in an hour.
Get up and put it on the table.
daily_drills.record_this: Pick up an apple, look at it, and put it in an hour — then turn off the light and get up.
daily_drills.self_check: Say 'an apple' fast — does it sound almost like 'a napple'? That's exactly right. That's the sound native speakers actually produce.

isolation.instructions: Fricative sounds (/s/ /z/ /f/ /v/) link especially smoothly onto a following vowel because they can be held and stretched right into the next sound.
isolation.instructions_ar: الأصوات الاحتكاكية (/s/ /z/ /f/ /v/) بتترابط بسلاسة جدًا مع الحرف المتحرك اللي بعدها لأنها ممكن تمتد وتوصل مباشرة للصوت اللي بعدها.

sentence_practice: This is easy — give it to one of us.
I love it, as usual — have a seat.
Have a look, this is of use to you.
daily_drills.record_this: This is easy — give it to one of us, and have a seat, because as usual, I love it.
daily_drills.self_check: Can you feel the /s/, /z/, /f/, or /v/ sound stretch smoothly into the vowel that follows, with zero gap?

isolation.instructions: Stop consonants also link onto a following vowel — the small burst of air that normally ends the sound instead gets 'released' into the next word's vowel.
isolation.instructions_ar: الأصوات الانفجارية كذلك بتترابط مع الحرف المتحرك اللي بعدها — نفس الهواء اللي عادة بيختم الصوت بيتحرر جوه الكلمة اللي بعدها.

sentence_practice: Stop it — that's not at all a big idea.
Look up in black and white — took a picture.
It's a big idea, not at all bad.
daily_drills.record_this: Stop it — that's a big idea, not at all bad. Look up, took a picture, in black and white.
daily_drills.self_check: Are the stop consonants releasing smoothly into the next vowel, rather than fully stopping with silence before the next word?

target_sounds: linking practice: short phrases at speed

isolation.instructions: Today, combine everything from Days 1-3 in short, common phrases you'll use every day in conversation.
isolation.instructions_ar: النهارده هنجمع كل حاجة من الأيام ١-٣ في جُمل قصيرة شائعة هتستخدمها كل يوم في المحادثة.

sentence_practice: Come on in and wait a minute — check it out!
What about it? Hang on a second.
Wait a minute, come on in and check it out.
daily_drills.record_this: Come on in, wait a minute — what about it? Hang on a second and check it out.
daily_drills.self_check: Time yourself saying these phrases. Linked, natural speech should take LESS time than saying each word separately with pauses.

isolation.instructions: Apply linking across an entire natural sentence, not just short phrases — this is the real test of whether the skill has become automatic.
isolation.instructions_ar: طبّق الربط على جملة طبيعية كاملة، مش بس عبارات قصيرة — ده الاختبار الحقيقي لو المهارة بقت تلقائية.
practice: I picked up an apple and ate it all up.
Turn off the light and give it a rest.
Look at us — we made it out okay.

sentence_practice: I picked up an apple and ate it all up.
Turn off the light and give it a rest.
Look at us — we made it out okay.
daily_drills.record_this: I picked up an apple, ate it all up, turned off the light, and gave it a rest — look at us, we made it out okay.
daily_drills.self_check: Does this whole sentence flow as connected 'chunks' of sound rather than a list of separate words?

daily_drills.target_sounds: Review: consonant+vowel linking with fricatives, stops, short phrases, full sentences
challenge_sentences: Pick up an apple, give it a look, and check it out — not at all a big idea, but come on in anyway.
This is easy — turn off the light, wait a minute, and look at us now.
Stop it, have a seat, and get up in an hour — that's all of it.
daily_drills.record_this: Come on in, have a seat — pick up an apple, give it a look, and wait a minute. This is easy, not at all a big idea, but check it out anyway.
daily_drills.self_check: Read the passage naturally. Underline (on paper) every place a consonant links into a following vowel. You should find at least 8 in this short passage.

test_yourself.instructions: Record this passage at a natural pace, focusing on linking every consonant-ending word into the next vowel-starting word.
test_yourself.passage: Every evening, I pick up an apple on my way home and eat it all up before dinner. It's not at all a big idea, but it helps me relax. Come on in, have a seat, and give it a try yourself — turn off your phone for an hour and just enjoy a quiet moment. Look at us now, we made it out of a busy day okay.

sounds_to_check.consonant+vowel linking (stops): pick up, eat it, not at all, big idea, made it, out of
sounds_to_check.linking in short phrases: come on in, have a seat, turn off, an hour

scoring_guide.90_100: Consistent, natural linking across the whole passage; sentences flow in connected chunks rather than isolated words.
scoring_guide.70_89: Linking present in most places; a few words still pronounced with unnatural pauses.
scoring_guide.50_69: Linking only in drilled/memorized phrases, not applied to new combinations.
scoring_guide.below_50: Words pronounced separately throughout — go back and repeat Days 1-5, focusing on fricatives and stops separately first.

for_arabic_speakers: This is blueprint Rule 1 of connected speech — the foundation for everything else in linking (Rules 2 and 3 come in later weeks).
If you're not sure whether to link two words, try saying the phrase FAST 3 times in a row — the natural link usually emerges on its own once you stop consciously controlling each word.
Don't worry about linking EVERY possible pair at first — prioritize very common phrases (pick up, look at, an apple) since you'll use these constantly.
supplementary_notes.recording_requirement: Record the 'record_this' sentence every day. By Day 7, aim for smooth, connected chunks rather than word-by-word delivery.
